sentences.text: My name is Harry Toporek.
T-O-P-O-R-E-K.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Not.
No, no, no.
Not whatsoever.
Tzvi-- it's.
I was born April the 20th, 1923, in Lask, Poland.
I'm 72-years-old today.
Lodz.
That was a big industrial city-- the name, Lodz.
It was about 32 kilometers from our town, from Lask.
My mother's name was Esther.
Her maiden name was Goldberg.
She was also born in Lask.
I don't know.
I don't remember the date.
His name was Moshe Toporek.
And he was 58 years old in 1942.
Also in Lask.
Lask was a small city with 350 Jewish families.
We were probably about 50% percent from the population before the war.
Too many rich people in the city were not.
But we all made a living, and somehow, we survive.
I remember that my parents, I mean, that they were struggling for making a-- ends meet-- let's put it this way.
This is what I remember from Lask until actually the war came in existence.
We had a tannery, but most of the people were-- they hardly made a living, let's put it this way.
We-- our family was not considered rich.
I mean-- never were.
You know, I know lots of people that tried to tell, you know, what they used to have at home.
Naturally, when they got in concentration camp, that little bit was not even there either.
So.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
We were six brothers.
My father had six-- eight children-- six sons and two daughters.
I came almost on the bottom, on the-- I was the youngest-- next to the youngest.
Between the oldest and the youngest, that was 15 years of a spread.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh, sure.
I know my brother, Efroim, he was the oldest.
Then came my sister, Frieda.
Then came my brother, Joe.
Then come my brother, Lazar.
Then came my brother, Leip.
Then came my brother, Sam.
Then I was born.
And after me, my father-- he was the lucky guy-- he got a little girl after six boys, you know.
And she was everything in the world for him.
This, I do remember.
That child was-- was his baby.
We all loved her dearly.
My parents were religious.
That wasn't something like not kosher to come in our house.
And my father was very active in the synagogue, you know.
And he was a very religious individual, yeah-- very.
My mother was even worse.
I mean, she was strictly religious.
Oh, sure, sure, sure.
There was only one synagogue-- the Lask Synagogue.
As a matter of fact, that was such a popular synagogue, that Mr.
Vishniac was interested to come in 1936, to Lask, and take pictures from the synagogue, and from the rabbi.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The photographer, right-- Roman Vishniac.
As a matter of fact, I got the book here.
I believe I showed that to you.
Yeah, sure.
That was Eisenberg-- Leibele Eisenberg, they used to call him.
E-I-S-E-N-B-E-R-G.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yes, I had one.
Mine oldest family, my brother, he belonged to the Bund.
He was very active in the Bund.
This was on the-- on the left side of the-- left-- left side from the middle, let's put it this way.
I personally belonged to the Betar.
This was the opposite side, you know?
And, listen, this is the way people used to live.
I mean, we used to have organization.
Each one belonged to a different organization.
Your-- your parents, which way did they go?
Left or-- or right?
Oh, no, no.
They were religious.
They didn't like what either one of us were doing.
[LAUGHTER]
Oh, we always used to have-- we always used to have the Jewish paper, the Polish paper.
And the elderly people-- the people, I mean, they-- for them, the-- the-- the news was the They were also interested in what goes on in the world.
Don't get me wrong.
They were not fanatic.
But the most important thing for them then, that was the And--
Yiddish.
Nothing but Yiddish.
We talked Polish.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Since Hitler came to power, in 1933, they came out.
The anti-Semitism was, in Poland, very, very strong.
They didn't like the Jews, either-- even before.
But that was somehow-- that was-- Pilsudski had the power.
I mean, he was the prime minister in Poland at that time.
And somehow, that was not-- that didn't came out until, really, Hitler came to power.
And they have seen what Hitler is trying to do with the Jews.
And to an extent, that-- that-- they were happy to see it.
They were very unfriendly to the Jewish people-- the Polish-- the Polacks, I mean.
No, I mean, you had signs that shouldn't-- nobody should buy by Jewish stores.
And--
Prior.
I mean, yeah, that was-- after Hitler, that was all over.
Wherever you went, I mean, you could see signs on every street.
So, you know--
--on --on the houses, on the-- wherever you went, I mean, you could see and [SPEAKING POLISH] I mean, that's mean that you shouldn't buy by-- by Jews.
Pardon me?
Yeah.
[SPEAKING POLISH] Don't buy by a Jew.
Do not buy by Jew-- over there.
Yeah, I mean, that was-- that-- that-- that-- for a slogan.
I mean, that the-- they should go to their own stores.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Both.
I finished public school, and then I mostly-- I went-- I went to a Jewish school.
We taught when somebody finished seven-- seven classes, for-- just like high school in here.
Yes, general education, right.
I would say so, yes.
The Jewish children went to separate classes.
We had separate schools.
Even the public schools.
Within the public schools, yes, they were separated.
Whatever the reason was-- I know that then we had a school that was Jews and non-Jews, but we had separate classes.
It was always frictions and fighting came out.
Yes, yes, yes, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh sure, oh sure.
I mean, that was every-- every-- daily-- occur-- that's happened every day, you know, that-- not in particular.
Today, like, that was very different today than it was two weeks ago.
But I always think what's happened.
You could feel that you're not wanted.
You're not liked.
You know, that was certain streets that Jewish boys or girls were afraid to go in there, because the gentile were living in there, and they didn't welcome.
We were not welcome in that street.
Stones were thrown at us.
And that was like, we-- we knew already in which direction, which areas we can go and be safe, and not safe-- like you got neighborhoods in here.
What I enjoyed doing is to-- to ride [LAUGHS] a bike, but I couldn't have it.
It was too expensive for me to have a bike.
It was playing with kids like any other children, you know, like children here.
And we had just as much fun.
Whatever we had, we enjoyed each other, and we had fun.
We were catching fish, you know, instead going to-- to-- to school, to go to Hebrew-- I mean, classes.
And it's the same thing.
Kids are kids wherever you go.
As I grew up, I have seen there was no future in Poland for Jewish people and for the young-- youth.
We will have to run away if the occasion-- I mean, if you're going to have a chance of running.
Like, for instance, people in France used to live a lot nicer and better.
In Germany, before Hitler came to power, people used to live more.
They had more freedom.
The anti-Semitism was not in Germany as embedded like it was by-- in Poland.
You see, in Germany, the anti-Semitism had to be brought out by Hitler.
In Poland, there were no need for it.
I remember, in 1933, I was about 10 years old.
And I used to read the Jewish paper.
I mean, it was very interested in what goes on in the world.
Maybe we have seen.
We all-- we didn't discuss.
We know.
We-- we had discussions about it, but we know the end result will not-- probably will not be a good one.
Because, you see, even if the war wouldn't break out, that anti-Semitism, what's happened in Germany, reflect the countries all the way around Germany.
And we were-- one-- one of the brothers-- we were only, let's say, certain-- certain places, we were only 50 miles or 40 kilometers for-- you know, from-- from the German border.
We knew exactly what was going on through radio and through the newspaper.
We all have seen the same thing.
And none of us could do anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Now, in 1939, September the 1st, the war did broke out.
And I remember, that was on a Friday.
And people used to come, maybe about 40 kilometers away from our town, that the city was bombarded even before war was declared.
So people actually came without anything on them, to run away from the bombs.
And we have seen, I mean, that there's a tragic thing approaching.
What the end gonna be, we only know that the war started.
How that's gonna end, where it's gonna end, and when it's gonna end, nobody knows.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How were-- how-- how was Hitler's-- how was the invasion at this time?
You talk about how the Jews felt about it.
How did the non-Jews feel about this?
When the Germans came into our town, the Polish population, some of them, pointed out where Jews are living.
Or if we stayed in line, some of the Polacks also pointed out the Jews are in line.
They-- they had to move.
They had to go back-- either to the back of the line, or they chased them away from the line.
I mean, they were not too friendly.
Not to us.
We didn't have any contact before the war with too many Poles.
And we haven't got any contact there.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
At any time?
Yes.
When the war broke out, on November the 11th-- that usually was a-- a holiday.
Because on November 11, the Poles were liberated after 125 years being under the Russian and the German occupation.
So that-- that was a day, like, we've got the 4th of July.
The Germans came, and they took the-- the rabbi and the Jewish people into the marktplatz.
That was a place.
And they used to make a comedy show.
You know, they used to beat the Jews.
They had to go on.
One was beating the other, you know, and running like horses around.
And the rabbi, was-- they gave him a goat, and he had to slaughter the goat.
I mean, they made just a-- a comedy show.
And they had fun.
Thanks God that we were not picked up.
I remember, I was with my brother on the attic-- hiding.
And we could see that they were schlepping the Jews out into the marktplatz to, you know, to make the performance.
And after that, we decided-- particularly me-- I decided that I-- we have to run.
If we want to save our life, we got to get out.
We also knew, at that time, that Poland has been divided-- 1/2 of Poland went to the Soviets, and 1/2 of Poland was occupied by the Germans.
So I went to the police station.
I took a-- a piece of paper that I am-- a document that I am looking for a brother in Grodno.
And Grodno wasn't under Russian occupation.
And then I took my brother with me, mine oldest brother, and another brother, Leip.
But he was too-- he was-- he got sick with his leg, so we left him in Lodz.
And he went back to Lask.
And we went to Russia.
We got into Bialystok.
At that time, that was no ghetto.
November, it was no-- no ghetto, I mean, at that time.
That just-- this-- this was only a preparation, you know?
Yeah.
When I went to Bialystok-- and I was in Bialystok until, I would say, a month or six weeks-- life was not too easy for us.
But we had somebody that we know, a Mr.
Tannenbaum.
He was very kind and very nice to us.
And my brother decided that I should go back and discuss the situation, and tell the-- my father, and what should we do next.
Because we cannot be there forever.
Because the Russians, what-- at that time, they're insist.
They would like us that we should accept their citizenship-- Russian citizenship papers-- and ship us out to Soviet Russia.
I mean, they didn't-- they-- they meant well, because there was no work, I mean.
But it was hundreds of thousands of Jews escaping from the Germans right there.
I mean, that was no place where to go.
But I was not successful crossing the border.
So I came back to Bialystok.
Coming back to Bialystok, I have seen that my brother was not there anymore, and he forgot to leave the address.
He probably assumed that I crossed the border and it's good that it's going.
So I was looking for him.
And I was looking for about six or seven weeks.
And I was very lonely-- no place where to stay, no place-- I mean, except for the Tannenbaum family.
And I decided to come back.
When I came back, I find my brother Efroim at home.
And he took-- already organized another group of people.
And he went back to Soviet Russia.
And I remained in the ghetto.
In Lask.
Then they made a ghetto.
They took the worst neighborhood in the city, and they put all the people, I mean, where to live in certain areas, you know?
They allocated a part-- this is the-- this is the place where Jews are gonna live.
And the rest of them had to go in.
That was in 19-- 1939.
Oh, sure.
Our street-- we used to live-- that was-- that-- that was part of the ghetto.
So we had an advantage that we remained in our own house.
Yeah, I mean, for the people what they used to live, let's say, in the better locations, or in a different locations, they were pushed in.
I mean, that-- they lost their homes.
They were only given two days.
And then we had to share with them the apartment.
They didn't even give us a break that we should take in the people what we know.
They just, within two days, they had to move.
So they had to pack in people wherever they could.
And that was actually generated by the Germans, that it should be friction between ourselves.
You see?
And that-- this created frictions.
Now, the-- the people that were in the ghetto now, were they just from your community of Lask?
Or were they--
Only.
So far, except there are-- there were certain people that they came from other towns, because they used to make judenrein certain areas not far from us.
Some often came to our town.
Oh, that was-- that used to be-- belonged to Germany before the war-- I mean, before the-- the first world war, like Poznan, Bydgoszcz.
And also, a lots of ghettos that feel they had family in Lask, they came.
A lots of people came to our ghettos, all right, by themself-- not forced in, but they came voluntarily.
I mean, they came because the-- their cities had to be wiped out of Jews, you know-- so ended up going someplace.
They used to ship 'em to Lublin, in the vicinity-- so ended up going there.
But if they have families, relative in our town, they came to Lask.
Poznan, Bydgoszcz--
--Kalisz.
Poznan is P-A-- oh-- P-O-S-N-A-N-- Poznan.
That was a big-- big city in Poland.
Oh, Kalisz-- K-A-L-I-S-S-- or S-Z.
Yeah.
That was-- I mean, those two places--
--is what they're-- actually, they cleaned them out.
They did want to have judenrein.
Right to 1939, in a way, they were better off, because there they had a chance to escape to Soviet Russia.
And the remainder, because there were no way to place to go for them back.
When I was in Soviet Russia, I-- I was-- I was missing my home.
For the people where they were liquidated, I mean, where they made judenrein, clear of Jews, they haven't got a home to go where.
I wish I wouldn't have a home to go back, and I would remain in Soviet Russia.
That was an open ghetto.
Nobody was watching us.
And nobody was running away.
If they caught you, after some-- if they catch somebody out of the ghetto, they were shot.
That wasn't no Polish police and no German police for-- for to guard us.
If we had to get out, we could only walk from 8 o'clock in the morning.
That was a curfew to about 6:00 or 7:00.
That all depends.
We had to remain.
We had to remain in the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Very bad.
For most of the people, what they came to our neighborhood, it was very, extremely bad.
We had to make kitchens.
I know lots of people went to public kitchens, you know, that was organized by the Jews and also the Germans.
The food came from the Germans.
We tried to go also in the evening for people that did not need to eat in the public places, to make 'em feel at home, you know-- that everybody joined in, you know?
Because it was very respectful people.
I mean, within-- within three, four months, they had nothing.
They had to leave their apartment with-- with-- within two hours, sometime a day.
So how many people-- well, you already had a large family.
How many people were in your home?
That was another family.
We used to have a room, a kitchen, and a bedroom.
They put in another family to us.
And there were five people in the other family.
Oh, we were living in lots of people.
I mean, we always used to sleep, you know, during the ghetto, at four, five people in one bed.
I mean, this-- this was a normal thing.
I mean, we had to take those people in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If we had something more, we used to share with people what didn't have.
My mother used to take care on those thing.
That was 2 and 1/2 when I--
